1: A shadow: 'I said in my heart, 'Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy yourself.' But behold, this also was vanity.' The self-experiment with pleasure — testing whether enjoyment can answer the vanity-question. Hebrews 4:15: 'we have a high priest who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.' Christ underwent the full range of human temptation (including the temptation to satisfy himself through pleasure, cf. Luke 4:1-13) without falling. Where Qohelet discovers that pleasure is vanity, Christ demonstrates that the proper response to temptation is not indulgence but faithfulness — 'man shall not live by bread alone' (Luke 4:4).
2: A shadow: 'I said of laughter, 'It is mad,' and of pleasure, 'What use is it?'' Qohelet's dismissal of laughter as madness and pleasure as purposeless. Luke 6:25: 'Woe to you who laugh now, for you will mourn and weep.' Christ's woe to hollow laughter stands with Qohelet's verdict. But the NT also promises the inversion: John 16:22: 'you will be sorrowful, but your sorrow will turn into joy... your joy no one will take from you.' The empty laughter Qohelet dismisses contrasts with the full joy Christ gives — not the laughter that is mad but the joy that is solid and permanent.
3: A shadow: 'I searched with my heart how to cheer my body with wine — my heart still guiding me with wisdom — and how to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was good for the children of man to do under heaven during the few days of their life.' Qohelet's search for legitimate pleasure through wine. John 2:1-11: Christ transforms water into wine at the wedding — not ending the pleasure of wine but providing better wine, a sign of the kingdom's abundance. But more: John 6:53-56 reframes the search entirely — the flesh and blood of Christ are the true food and drink, the thing Qohelet was searching for when he reached for wine. The experiment with wine points toward the one who gives wine as a sign of his blood poured out for the joy of the world.
4: A shadow: 'I made great works. I built houses and planted vineyards for myself.' Solomon as the great builder — the human capacity for construction pushed to its maximum. The ultimate Builder is Christ, who builds what will not fail: 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matthew 16:18). And the eschatological construction project: Revelation 21:1-2 — the new Jerusalem coming down from God, prepared as a bride. The city that Qohelet builds on earth and then leaves behind becomes in Christ the city that descends from heaven and endures forever.
5: A shadow: 'I made myself gardens and parks, and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees.' The garden-paradise that Solomon constructs — the attempt to recreate Eden through human ingenuity. The trajectory of Scripture runs: Eden lost (Gen 3) → wilderness (Sinai) → promised land → the perfect garden of Song of Solomon 4:12-16 → the new creation's garden-city (Revelation 22:1-5). Christ is the one who reopens the gate of paradise: 'today you will be with me in paradise' (Luke 23:43). The garden that Qohelet builds with his own hands anticipates the garden Christ reopens at the cross and perfects in the new creation.
6: A shadow: 'I made myself pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.' The water-provision for the constructed garden — Solomon making himself the source of water for his project. Jesus at the well: 'whoever drinks of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again. The water that I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life' (John 4:14). The pools Qohelet digs to water his gardens are an image of the human attempt to secure the life-giving resource; Christ offers the water that is inexhaustible and self-replenishing, not stored in pools but welling up within.
7: A shadow: 'I bought male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house. I had also great possessions of herds and flocks, more than any who had been before me in Jerusalem.' The accumulation of possessions — Solomon at the peak of material wealth. 2 Corinthians 8:9: 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.' Christ's voluntary impoverishment is the inversion of Qohelet's wealth-accumulation. Where Solomon accumulated everything and found it vanity, Christ gave up everything and in that giving secured an imperishable inheritance (1 Peter 1:4) for those who were impoverished.
8: A shadow: 'I also gathered for myself silver and gold and the treasure of kings and provinces.' The treasury-accumulation — the maximum of material wealth. 2 Corinthians 8:9: Christ 'though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor.' Colossians 2:3: 'in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge.' The silver and gold Qohelet gathers and will leave behind are the shadow of the imperishable treasure Christ is and gives. Matthew 6:19-20: 'Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven.' The Qohelet experiment demonstrates why earthly treasure cannot satisfy; Christ provides the alternative.
9: A shadow: 'So I became great and surpassed all who were before me in Jerusalem. Also my wisdom remained with me.' Solomon at his zenith — the human greatness that has no peer. Philippians 2:9: 'Therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name.' But the path to Christ's exaltation is through humiliation, not accumulation — precisely the opposite of Qohelet's ascending greatness-narrative. The greatness that Christ achieves through self-giving endures; the greatness Qohelet achieves through accumulation passes away. Paul's 'therefore God highly exalted him' follows the cross, not the treasury.
10: A shadow: 'And whatever my eyes desired I did not keep from them. I kept my heart from no pleasure, for my heart found pleasure in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.' The unrestrained self-indulgence of the pleasure-experiment. Hebrews 4:15: 'we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin.' Where Qohelet withheld nothing from himself, Christ — facing every temptation — withheld himself from sin. The contrast is not asceticism vs. pleasure but faithfulness vs. indulgence. Christ's 'yet without sin' is what makes his high priesthood effective where Qohelet's experiment failed.
11: A theme: 'Then I considered all that my hands had done and the toil I had expended in doing it, and behold, all was vanity and a striving after wind, and there was nothing to be gained under the sun.' The comprehensive verdict on human achievement: all vanity. 1 Corinthians 15:58: 'Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain.' The transformation from Qohelet's 'all is vanity' to Paul's 'not in vain' is entirely dependent on the resurrection of Christ. The 'under the sun' qualifier in Qohelet's verdict signals the limitation: within the merely temporal frame, it is true. But Christ breaks the temporal frame.
12: A shadow: 'So I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly, for what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.' The wisdom-madness-folly survey — Qohelet investigating the full range of human mental life. 1 Corinthians 1:18-25: 'the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God... God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise.' Paul inverts the wisdom-folly hierarchy in light of the cross: what seems wisdom (Qohelet's rational investigation) cannot reach what only revelation reveals; what seems folly (the cross) is actually the wisdom of God.
13: A direct connection: 'Then I saw that there is more gain in wisdom than in folly, as there is more gain in light than in darkness.' The light-exceeds-darkness observation — even Qohelet recognizes that wisdom has relative advantage. John 1:5: 'The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' What Qohelet observes as a general truth — light exceeds darkness — Christ claims as a personal identity. The relative advantage of light over darkness becomes in Christ the absolute: 'the darkness has not overcome it.'
14: A direct connection: 'The wise person has his eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. And yet I perceived that the same event happens to all of them.' The sight-blindness contrast — the wise man uses his eyes; the fool is in darkness. John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' Christ addresses not just the preference for wisdom over folly but the source of the light that enables sight. The 'same event happens to all' — both wise and fool die — is the limitation Qohelet sees but cannot transcend; the resurrection is the event that breaks the 'same fate for all' principle.
15: A type: 'Then I said in my heart, 'What happens to the fool will happen to me also. Why then have I been so very wise?' And I said in my heart that this also is vanity.' Qohelet's recognition that the same fate overtakes the wise as the fool — death equalizes. Hebrews 2:14-17: 'Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death... Therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect.' Christ deliberately shared the fate of the fool and the wise alike — mortality — in order to destroy death from the inside. The 'same event happens to all' is what Christ entered, and in entering he undid it.
16: A shadow: 'For of the wise as of the fool there is no enduring remembrance, seeing that in the days to come all will have been long forgotten. How the wise dies just like the fool!' The amnesia that overtakes even the wise — no permanent memorial. John 14:26: 'the Helper, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in my name, he will teach you all things and bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you.' The Spirit's role of 'bringing to remembrance' counters the amnesia principle. And Psalms 112:6, cited in the NT tradition: 'the righteous will be remembered forever.' What Qohelet sees as the universal amnesia-fate is reversed for those in Christ, whose words and deeds are preserved by the Spirit.
17: A direct allusion: 'So I hated life, because what is done under the sun was grievous to me, for all is vanity and a striving after wind.' Qohelet's life-hatred — the nadir of the wisdom experiment. Mark 14:34: 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death' (Gethsemane). Matthew 26:38: 'remain here, and watch with me.' Christ in Gethsemane enters the depth of grief that Qohelet describes — the soul overwhelmed by the weight of existence. But where Qohelet's life-hatred produces despair, Christ's agony produces surrender: 'not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42). The pattern: enter the grief fully, then surrender it to the Father.
18: A theme: 'I hated all my toil in which I toil under the sun, seeing that I must leave it to the man who comes after me.' The grief of leaving accumulated toil to an unknown heir — the pointlessness of accumulation. Galatians 4:4: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son.' The 'fullness of time' means that all the toil of history — the generations of patriarchs, judges, kings, prophets — had its accumulation directed toward a specific moment. Christ does not leave his work to an uncertain heir; he gives it to his church through the Spirit, and the inheritance is imperishable (1 Peter 1:3-5). The toil that Qohelet leaves to an unknown stranger, Christ bequeaths through deliberate covenant.
19: A shadow: 'and who knows whether he will be wise or a fool? Yet he will be master of all for which I toiled and used my wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.' The unknown-heir problem — all accumulated wisdom will be managed by someone who may be a fool. Romans 8:28: 'we know that for those who love God all things work together for good, for those who are called according to his purpose.' The uncertainty Qohelet feels about what comes after him is replaced in Christ's framework by a purposive God who works all things toward the good. The apparently random distribution of wisdom's fruits is governed by the one who 'works all things according to the counsel of his will' (Ephesians 1:11).
20: A shadow: 'So I turned about and gave my heart up to despair over all the toil of my labors under the sun.' The despair that follows the recognition of toil's futility — Qohelet at his lowest. John 12:27: 'Now is my soul troubled. And what shall I say? 'Father, save me from this hour'? But for this purpose I have come to this hour.' Christ's 'troubled soul' before the cross is the weight of bearing the full accumulation of human toil-and-vanity. He enters Qohelet's despair, carries it to the cross, and through the resurrection transforms despair into 'it is finished' (John 19:30) — the toil accomplished, not abandoned.
21: A shadow: 'because sometimes a person who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill must leave everything to be enjoyed by someone who did not toil for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.' The injustice of leaving earned inheritance to an unearned heir. John 17:22, 24: 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them... Father, I desire that they also, whom you have given me, may be with me where I am, to see my glory.' Christ gives to his disciples — who did not earn it — the glory that he earned through his toil and suffering. The injustice Qohelet laments (earning it, another enjoys it) is reversed in the gift of grace: Christ earned it, his people receive it — not as injustice but as the deliberate generosity of the one who toiled for others.
22: A theme: 'For what does man get from all the toil and striving of heart with which he labors under the sun?' The yitron-question returned — what is the net gain? 1 Corinthians 15:58: 'your labor in the Lord is not in vain.' The answer to Qohelet's question depends entirely on the frame: under the sun (the purely temporal), the net gain is zero. In the Lord (the resurrection frame), labor has eternal weight. The question Qohelet asks is the right question; the answer he cannot yet supply is given by the one who rose from the dead and calls people to labor in his name.
23: A shadow: 'For all his days are full of sorrow, and his work is a vexation. Even in the night his heart does not rest. This also is vanity.' The sleepless grief of the toiling person — sorrow by day, no rest by night. Isaiah 53:3-4: 'He was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief... Surely he has borne our griefs and carried our sorrows.' Christ bears the cumulative weight of human sorrow — including the sorrow Qohelet describes — in his passion. Matthew 26:36-46: the sleepless night in Gethsemane, sweating blood, is the maximum of sorrow-with-no-rest-by-night. He entered the condition fully in order to bear it away.
24: A direct connection: 'There is nothing better for a person than that he should eat and drink and find enjoyment in his toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God.' Qohelet's positive conclusion: enjoyment is God's gift. John 6:53-55: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you. Whoever feeds on my flesh and drinks my blood has eternal life, and I will raise him up on the last day.' Christ takes the 'eat and drink' that Qohelet commends as God's best gift and transforms it into the Eucharist — the eating and drinking that gives eternal life. What Qohelet sees as the highest good God gives becomes in Christ the vehicle for the highest good of all: communion with the Son of God.
25: A direct connection: 'for apart from him who can eat or who can have enjoyment?' Nothing is truly enjoyed apart from God — Qohelet's theological claim about the source of enjoyment. John 6:27, 35: 'Do not work for the food that perishes, but for the food that endures to eternal life, which the Son of Man will give to you... I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst.' Christ is both the one who gives the food and the food itself — the 'apart from whom nothing is enjoyed' of Ecclesiastes becomes the 'I am the bread of life' of the gospel. The theological grammar is the same: all true enjoyment is from and through God; in Christ, that God has become accessible.
26: A direct allusion: 'For to the one who pleases him God has given wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner he has given the business of gathering and collecting, only to give to one who pleases God. This also is vanity and a striving after wind.' Divine favor as the source of wisdom, knowledge, and joy. Matthew 3:17; Ecclesiastes 17:5: 'This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.' Christ is the one whom the Father explicitly pleases — and to him, by right, belongs all wisdom, knowledge, and joy. The one who pleases God in Ecclesiastes 2:26 is first and perfectly Christ; believers enter into this pleased-with status through union with him (Ephesians 1:6: 'he has blessed us in the Beloved'). The distribution of wisdom to 'the one who pleases him' finds its ultimate referent in the Son in whom the Father is well pleased.
